title: What Is HVE Core?
description: GitHub Copilot ships as a general-purpose assistant. HVE Core transforms it into a constraint-based engineering workflow engine with specialized agents, auto-applied instructions, reusable prompts, and validated skills.
objectives: Identify the problem HVE Core solves for teams using GitHub Copilot
Distinguish the four artifact types that compose the HVE Core library
Describe collection-driven architecture and how it organizes artifacts into distributable products
Recognize the range of teams and workflows HVE Core supports
talkingPoints: The Problem HVE Core Solves: Copilot alone has no memory of team conventions, no enforcement of standards, and no structured workflows
The Four Artifact Types: agents, instructions, prompts, and skills each serve a distinct role
Collection-Driven Architecture: the collection is the organizational and distribution unit
Who Uses HVE Core: from solo developers to enterprise platform teams

title: Installation and First Workflow
description: Three installation modes cover every deployment scenario — full marketplace install, selective collection install, and direct repo clone. This module walks through each option and runs a first agent conversation.
objectives: Compare the three installation modes and select the appropriate one for your team
Execute a complete installation from the VS Code Marketplace
Launch a first agent conversation in Copilot Chat and observe artifact activation
Verify the installation using the validation checklist
talkingPoints: Three Installation Modes: HVE Core All for full library, HVE Installer for selective collections, repo clone for contributors
Step-by-Step Marketplace Install: one-click install from the VS Code Marketplace
First Agent Conversation: select an agent from the picker and start a task
Verification: confirm agents, instructions, and prompts are active

title: The RPI Methodology
description: Research, Plan, Implement is the structured methodology that turns Copilot from a freeform assistant into an evidence-first engineering workflow. Each phase has a dedicated agent, a defined output, and a clear handoff contract.
objectives: Define the three phases of the RPI methodology and the output each phase produces
Trace the data flow between the Task Researcher, Task Planner, and Task Implementor agents
Explain how the Task Reviewer closes the feedback loop and enables iterative refinement
Identify when to use the full RPI cycle versus individual phase agents
talkingPoints: The Research Phase: evidence gathering before planning prevents the most common AI failure mode — acting on assumptions
The Plan Phase: transforms research findings into an actionable implementation plan
The Implement Phase: executes the plan with constraint enforcement from instructions
The Review Phase: the Task Reviewer closes the loop with structured feedback

title: Collections — The Product Boundary
description: Collections are the organizational and distribution unit in HVE Core. Each collection groups related artifacts into a distributable product with manifest metadata, maturity levels, and packaging rules.
objectives: Define what a collection is and identify its two required files
Map the 13 collections to their functional domains
Distinguish stable, preview, and experimental maturity levels and their distribution implications
Explain how the packaging pipeline uses collection manifests to build extensions
talkingPoints: What a Collection Is: a manifest YAML plus a description markdown that define a distributable bundle
The 13 Collections: from hve-core and coding-standards to design-thinking and security
The Maturity Model: stable ships to production, preview is opt-in, experimental is internal
Packaging Pipeline: collections drive extension builds through artifact discovery and dependency closure

title: Agents — Specialized AI Assistants
description: Agents are markdown files with YAML frontmatter that give Copilot persistent personas, scoped capabilities, and tool access. HVE Core ships 49 agents organized across 10 collection groupings.
objectives: Describe the agent file format including frontmatter fields and system prompt body
Identify the agent taxonomy across collection groupings — RPI, planning, review, generators, and platforms
Explain the subagent architecture and how parent agents reference child agents through glob-based resolution
Use the Copilot Chat agent picker to select and invoke specialized agents
talkingPoints: Agent File Format: a .agent.md file with YAML frontmatter declaring description, tools, model, and subagents
Agent Taxonomy: 49 agents across RPI workflow, planning, review, code generation, and platform integration
Subagent Architecture: parent agents delegate to specialized child agents via glob-based references
Agent Picker: VS Code surfaces installed agents in the Copilot Chat picker for direct invocation

title: Instructions — Auto-Applied Guidelines
description: Instructions are markdown files that Copilot loads automatically based on which files are open in the editor. They enforce coding standards, architectural constraints, and team conventions without explicit invocation.
objectives: Explain how instructions auto-apply through applyTo glob patterns matched against open files
Map the instruction hierarchy from shared guidelines to language-specific coding standards
Describe how multiple instruction files can match a single open file and how they compose
Trace how frontmatter schema validation enforces structural correctness
talkingPoints: The Auto-Apply Mechanism: instructions activate when open files match their applyTo glob pattern
Directory Organization: 102 instruction files organized into shared and language-specific layers
Pattern Composition: multiple instructions can match the same file, building a layered context
Schema Validation: every instruction file's frontmatter is validated against a JSON Schema in CI

title: Prompts — Reusable Task Templates
description: Prompts are reusable task templates that surface as slash commands in Copilot Chat. They encode multi-step workflows, onboarding sequences, and standardized procedures into invocable templates.
objectives: Define prompts as reusable task templates and distinguish their role from agents and instructions
Identify the prompt file format including frontmatter fields and the agent delegation field
Map prompt families across functional domains — onboarding, git workflows, security, and platform integrations
Invoke prompts via slash commands in Copilot Chat
talkingPoints: Prompts vs Agents vs Instructions: agents carry personas, instructions carry constraints, prompts carry task workflows
Prompt File Format: a .prompt.md file with description, mode, and optional agent delegation
Prompt Families: 63 prompts organized by domain — onboarding, git, ADO, security, and more
Slash Command Invocation: prompts surface as /prompt-name in Copilot Chat for direct use

title: Skills — Self-Contained Packages
description: Skills bundle domain expertise with executable scripts, reference documents, test suites, and cross-platform tooling into self-contained packages that agents and Copilot load on demand.
objectives: Describe skill anatomy including the SKILL.md entry point, scripts, references, tests, and assets
Explain Python skill requirements including pyproject.toml, ruff configuration, and pytest integration
Trace the cross-platform scripting pattern with Bash and PowerShell implementations validated by CI
Identify the 11 skills shipped with HVE Core and their domain coverage
talkingPoints: Skill Anatomy: a directory anchored by SKILL.md with optional scripts, references, tests, and assets
Python Requirements: pyproject.toml, ruff linting, fuzz harness, and pytest integration for Python skills
Cross-Platform Scripts: both Bash and PowerShell implementations validated by CI for every scripted skill
The 11 Skills: coding-standards, security, experimental, and platform-specific skill packages

title: Writing Custom Artifacts
description: This module covers the complete lifecycle of authoring custom instructions, agents, and prompts — from file creation through frontmatter configuration, collection manifest updates, and validation.
objectives: Construct instruction files with valid frontmatter and targeted applyTo patterns
Define agent files with system prompts that produce consistent, scoped behavior
Create prompt files with correct frontmatter and optional agent delegation
Integrate new artifacts into collection manifests and run the validation pipeline
talkingPoints: Custom Instructions: frontmatter schema, applyTo targeting, layered composition
Custom Agents: system prompt authoring, subagent references, tool declarations
Custom Prompts: slash command creation, agent field delegation, mode configuration
Validation Pipeline: lint:all checks, collection manifest updates, plugin regeneration

title: The Design Thinking Framework
description: The Design Thinking collection implements a nine-method coaching framework as an AI-assisted workflow. A DT Coach agent guides teams through structured problem discovery and solution development, loading method-specific knowledge on demand.
objectives: Trace the nine-method sequence through Problem Space, Solution Space, and Implementation Space
Distinguish method-tier and deep-tier instruction architecture for on-demand knowledge loading
Apply industry context files to adapt coaching for different domains
Describe the DT-to-RPI handoff that bridges design thinking into implementation
talkingPoints: Nine-Method Coaching System: three spaces with three methods each guide structured problem solving
Two-Tier Instruction Architecture: method-tier for overview, deep-tier for detailed guidance loaded on demand
Industry Adaptation: context files for Manufacturing, Healthcare, Energy, and other domains
DT-to-RPI Handoff: design thinking outputs feed directly into the RPI implementation workflow

title: Security Planning Agents
description: HVE Core ships three planning agents that transform security and Responsible AI assessment from ad-hoc review into structured, repeatable workflows — STRIDE threat modeling, NIST AI RMF assessment, and OpenSSF supply chain scoring.
objectives: Trace the Security Planner's six-phase STRIDE-based threat modeling workflow
Describe the RAI Planner's Responsible AI assessment against NIST AI RMF 1.0
Explain the SSSC Planner's supply chain security assessment using OpenSSF Scorecard and SLSA
Map the shared phased workflow architecture with state persistence and backlog handoff
talkingPoints: Security Planner: six-phase STRIDE threat modeling with structured finding output
RAI Planner: Responsible AI assessment against NIST AI Risk Management Framework
SSSC Planner: supply chain security scoring using OpenSSF Scorecard, SLSA, and Sigstore
Shared Architecture: phased workflows with state persistence, dual-format backlog handoff

title: The Validation Infrastructure
description: HVE Core contains over 500 artifacts validated by 15-plus specialized linters. The lint:all pipeline chains frontmatter validation, markdown linting, YAML checking, PowerShell analysis, dependency pinning, and collection metadata verification.
objectives: Enumerate the specialized linters and describe what each validates across the artifact library
Explain the frontmatter schema validation system including JSON Schema mapping and the validation script
Describe how lint:all chains validation passes into a comprehensive quality gate
Identify how the validation infrastructure prevents structural entropy in a large prompt library
talkingPoints: The Linter Inventory: 15-plus validation passes covering markdown, YAML, PowerShell, Python, and frontmatter
Frontmatter Schema Validation: JSON Schemas mapped to artifact types enforce structural correctness
The lint:all Pipeline: chained validation from formatting through security pinning checks
Preventing Structural Entropy: automated validation keeps 500-plus artifacts consistent as the library grows

title: Extension Packaging and CI/CD
description: The extension packaging pipeline transforms collection manifests into distributable VS Code extensions. The CI/CD architecture uses 48 GitHub Actions workflows organized by concern — validation, security, testing, release, and maintenance.
objectives: Describe the two-step Prepare and Package pipeline for extension builds
Explain collection-scoped versus full builds and how maturity filtering controls what ships
Map the CI/CD workflow categories and the orchestrator pattern that coordinates them
Trace the release pipeline from release-please through SBOM generation and marketplace publishing
talkingPoints: Prepare and Package: artifact discovery with dependency closure, then VSIX bundling
Collection-Scoped Builds: HVE Core All includes everything, individual collections ship subsets
CI/CD Architecture: 48 workflows in five categories orchestrated by three top-level dispatchers
Release Automation: release-please, extension packaging, SBOM, attestation, marketplace publishing

title: Enterprise Adoption and Contributing
description: Scaling HVE Core across enterprise teams requires collection-scoped deployment, governance alignment, and contributor onboarding. This module covers adoption strategy, the governance model, and the contribution workflow.
objectives: Design a collection-scoped deployment strategy for teams with different technology stacks
Apply the governance model — maintainer roles, decision tiers, inactivity policies
Execute the contribution pipeline from fork through validation and pull request submission
Assess HVE Core's strengths and current limitations for realistic enterprise adoption planning
talkingPoints: Collection-Scoped Deployment: teams install only the collections relevant to their stack and workflow
Governance Model: corporate-sponsored maintainer model with tiered decision authority
Contribution Workflow: fork, branch, validate locally with lint:all, submit PR with Conventional Commits
Honest Assessment: strengths in structured AI workflows, limitations in runtime enforcement and telemetry
